title: Write to a file
successMessage: ofstream creates the file if it doesn't exist, overwrites if it does. ios::app appends. The block-scoped ofstream is RAII — the file is closed when the block ends.

title: Read from a file — line by line
instruction:
`ifstream` reads from a file. `getline(file, line)` reads one line.

`while (getline(file, line))` loops until end of file.
successMessage: getline(ss, name, ',') reads up to the comma delimiter — perfect for CSV parsing. The stringstream splits each line. This is the pattern for every CSV reader.

title: Read word by word with >>
instruction:
`>>` on ifstream reads one whitespace-separated token at a time — like `cin`.

Perfect for structured data where items are separated by spaces.
successMessage: while (in >> word) reads until EOF. The map counts frequencies and iterates alphabetically. Word frequency counting from a file — every search engine starts here.

title: Check stream state
instruction:
Always check if a file opened successfully:

```cpp
if (!file) { cerr << "Error!"; return 1; }
```

Stream state flags:
- `good()` — all OK
- `eof()` — reached end of file
- `fail()` — formatting or logic error
- `bad()` — severe error
expectedOutput: Error: error: could not open file
successMessage: is_open() and the stream bool conversion both check if the file opened. fail() is true when the open failed. Always check before reading — an unchecked failed open produces silent garbage data.

title: filesystem — modern path handling
instruction:
`<filesystem>` (C++17) provides cross-platform file system operations:

```cpp
#include <filesystem>
namespace fs = filesystem;

fs::exists("file.txt")
fs::create_directories("path/to/dir")
fs::file_size("file.txt")
```
successMessage: filesystem provides cross-platform path operations — no more Windows vs Linux path separator issues. p.filename(), p.parent_path(), p.stem(), p.extension() decompose paths cleanly.

title: Build a log file system
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a `Logger` class:
- Constructor takes a filename, opens the file in append mode
- `log(string level, string message)` — writes `"[YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS] [LEVEL] message"`
- Use `<chrono>` or just write a fixed timestamp for simplicity
- `getLineCount()` — read the file and count lines
- Destructor closes the file

Log 5+ messages, then print the line count.
hint: Use ctime and localtime to format the timestamp, or write a fixed-format string.

codeChecks.message: Use ofstream or fstream for the log file.
Add log(string level, string message) method.
Add a destructor that closes the file.

successMessage: A RAII-based logger — destructor closes the file automatically. Append mode means logs accumulate across runs. This is how real logging libraries work. Chapter 19 done!
